objections.objection: Cost list collapses transition (years 1–4) and steady-state recurring lines and provides no quantitative ranges or per-school/per-pupil scalars. For example the scenario cites a one-off ‘transition cost years 1–4’ for teacher-training and an ongoing ‘teacher retention incentive’ (Magyar Falu Program: 5 billion HUF for 100 settlements) but gives no estimate of (a) the per-teacher or per-school training cost; (b) how many teachers/settlements must be funded; (c) the annual recurring budget required to sustain retention incentives if rural vacancies follow the dossier’s stated ageing/retirement profile. This omission prevents any fiscal-sensitivity analysis to teacher-retirement rates, training take-up, or small-change effects (e.g., a 10% higher vacancy rate).
objections.suggested_revision: Separate transition (years 1–4) and steady-state cost lines explicitly. Provide ballpark ranges and per-unit scalars (HUF per teacher trained; HUF per settlement housing incentive; HUF per multigrade class start-up kit) using available Hungarian datapoints (Magyar Falu Program budgets, teacher-payroll averages from KSH, OFI in-service training unit costs). Produce a sensitivity table that re-runs total annual cost under at least three teacher-supply scenarios (baseline, −10% supply, −25% supply) and at least two uptake rates for training (50%/80%). Report both nominal and 10–20 year NPV at 3 discount rates to show fiscal exposure and transition vs steady-state budget paths.

objections.objection: Transport and capital-investment items are under-specified and scaled from a single-case anecdote without appropriate scaling logic. The scenario cites a Délegyháza route (~35 million HUF/year for ~50 students) and notes rural per-student bus costs ~40% higher than urban, but provides no model to scale to national numbers (number of routes, average route length, occupancy rates) or to capture transitional double-running costs (simultaneous operation of village sites and receiving ISCED2 sites during construction/phase-in). It also omits financing costs (interest, debt-service schedules) and temporary capacity costs (e.g., renting interim classrooms, hiring short-term staff) that commonly inflate early-year budgets.
objections.suggested_revision: Add an explicit transport-cost model: parameterize cost per route-km, deadhead-km share, average students/route, and driver/staff costs; calibrate using Délegyháza as one datapoint but validate against KSH rural-transport and municipal-expenditure data to produce national-scale ranges. Include capital-cost ranges for ISCED2 expansion with financing scenarios (cash vs debt; 10- to 30-year amortization) and model transition-period double-running costs (estimate % of consolidated-capacity needed during construction). Run sensitivity analyses for low/medium/high ridership and two financing mixes to show fiscal sensitivity and peak-year budget needs.

objections.objection: The scenario acknowledges major long-run community fiscal externalities (property-value decline, municipal tax-base shrinkage, population-decline effects) but leaves them unquantified, despite citing evidence that those effects can exceed closure savings. Also missing are quantified repurposing/abandonment costs per closed school and explicit teacher severance/redeployment cost estimates tied to teacher-contract rules. Without monetizing these items or testing discount-rate/time-horizon sensitivity, the claim that consolidation ‘reduces structural per-pupil cost diseconomy’ is unsupported for budget decision-making.
objections.suggested_revision: Monetize long-run municipal fiscal impacts using a defensible counterfactual: estimate NPV of municipal tax-revenue loss per closure using Danish/Finnish effect sizes scaled to Hungarian municipal tax bases, and include property-value depreciation scenarios (10/20/30 year windows). Add per-school repurposing cost estimates (refurbish vs decommission) and model the share likely to remain vacant (use Portugal ~20% as baseline) with associated maintenance/security costs. Quantify teacher severance/redeployment in accordance with national labour/collective agreements (HUF per teacher, pension/tapering costs). Present aggregated NPV of consolidation under multiple time horizons (5/10/20 years) and discount rates to show when/if consolidation yields net public-budget savings.

objections.objection: Registry and oversight costs are stated qualitatively but lack allocation of who pays, specific cost ranges, and omission of foreseeable legal/GDPR compliance and litigation-defense costs if disaggregated Roma/non-Roma data are published. The scenario lists ‘independent-oversight/audit costs’ as distinct but does not budget ongoing enforcement/compliance and contingency costs for political discontinuities (registry underfunding after an election) nor model the fiscal responsibility split across ministry, KSH, and tankerület levels.
objections.suggested_revision: Specify the responsible budget owner for each cost line (Ministry of Education, KSH, independent auditor) and provide one-off and annual cost ranges (design/build, annual ops, audit) using comparable Hungarian IT/data projects as benchmarks. Include a GDPR/legal-compliance line (data‑protection officers, anonymization, legal defense reserve) and model a contingency for 0/50/100% funding continuity across electoral cycles to show fiscal risk. Recommend publishing a proposed budget line in the draft legislation and include staged funding triggers tied to registry milestones to reduce underfunding risk.
